title: ­ØśČ-substitution: indefinite integrals
description: Find indefinite integrals that require using the method of ­ØśČ-substitution.

hints: Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{1+(e^x)^2}\cdot \,e^x\, dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=e^x}$, then $\purpleD{du=e^x \, dx}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{1+(\blueD{e^x})^2}\,\cdot \purpleD{e^x\, dx}\,=\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{1+\blueD u^2}\, \purpleD{du}\,$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= \int\dfrac{1}{1+u^2}\,du~\\\\\\
&=~ \tan^{-1}u+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=~\tan^{-1}u+C\\\\\\\
&=~\tan^{-1}(e^x)+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx\,=~\tan^{-1}(e^x)+C$

If we let $\blueD {u=2x-5}$, then ${du=2 \, dx}$ and $\purpleD{dx=\dfrac{du}{2}}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int(\blueD{2x-5})^{10}\,\purpleD {dx}\,&=\displaystyle \int \blueD u^{10}\,\cdot \purpleD{\dfrac{du}{2}}\,\\\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int u^{10}\cdot \dfrac12\, du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac12\displaystyle \int  u^{10}\,du\,\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&=\dfrac12\displaystyle \int  u^{10}\,du\,\\\\\\
&=\dfrac12\cdot \dfrac{u^{11}}{11}+C\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{u^{11}}{22}+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=\dfrac{u^{11}}{22}+C\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{(2x-5)^{11}}{22}+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int(2x-5)^{10}dx\,=\dfrac{(2x-5)^{11}}{22}+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle\int(x^3+1)^6 \cdot 3x^2dx\,$

If we let $\blueD {u=x^3+1}$, then $\purpleD{du=3x^2 \, dx}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\displaystyle\int(\blueD{x^3+1})^6 \cdot \purpleD{3x^2dx}\,=\displaystyle \int \blueD {u}^6\, \purpleD{du}\,$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= \int u^6\,du~\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{u^7}{7}+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=\dfrac{u^7}{7}+C\\\\\\&=\dfrac{(x^3+1)^7}{7}+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int3x^2(x^3+1)^6dx\,=\dfrac{(x^3+1)^7}{7}+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle\int\sqrt{1-x^2}\,\cdot 8x \,dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=1-x^2}$, then ${du=-2x \, dx}$ and $\purpleD{x\,dx=-\dfrac{du}{2}}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int\sqrt{\blueD{1-x^2}}\,\cdot 8\purpleD{x \,dx}\,&=\displaystyle \int\sqrt{\blueD u}\,\,\cdot 8 \left(\purpleD{-\dfrac{du}{2}}\right)\,\\\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int\sqrt{ u} \cdot -4\,du \\\\\\\\
&=-4\displaystyle \int  \sqrt{u}\,du\,\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= -4\displaystyle \int\sqrt{ u}\, du\\\\\\
&=-4\cdot \dfrac23u\^{\large\frac32}+C\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{8}{3}u\^{\large\frac32}+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=-\dfrac{8}{3}u\^{\large\frac32}+C\\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{8}{3}(1-x^2)\^{\large\frac32}+C\end{align}$

The answer:

 $\displaystyle\int8x\sqrt{1-x^2}\, \,dx\,=-\dfrac{8}{3}(1-x^2)\^{\large\frac32}+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle \int (\ln x)^2\cdot \,\dfrac1x\, dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=\ln(x)}$, then $\purpleD{du=\dfrac1x \, dx}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\displaystyle \int (\blueD{\ln x})^2\cdot \,\purpleD{\dfrac1x\, dx}\,=\displaystyle \int \blueD u^2\, \purpleD{du}\,$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= \displaystyle \int  u^2\, {du}\,\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{u^3}{3}+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=~\dfrac{u^3}{3}+C\\\\\\\
&=~\dfrac{(\ln x)^3}{3}+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{(\ln{x})^2}{x}\,dx\,=~\dfrac{(\ln x)^3}{3}+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{x^3+4}\,\cdot x^2\,dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=x^3+4}$, then ${du=3x^2 \, dx}$ and $\purpleD{\dfrac{du}{3}=x^2 \, dx} $ 

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\blueD{x^3+4}}\,\cdot \purpleD{x^2\,dx}\,
&=\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\blueD u}\,\cdot \purpleD{\dfrac{du}{3}}\\\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\ u}\,\cdot \dfrac13\,du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac13\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\ u}\,du\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= \dfrac13\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\ u}\,du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{3}\ln|u|+C\\\\\\\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=\dfrac13\ln|u|+C\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac13\ln|x^3+4|+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{x^2}{x^3+4}\,dx\,= \dfrac13\ln|x^3+4|+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle \int e^{x^4}\cdot \,x^3\, dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=x^4}$, then ${du=4x^3 \, dx}$ and $\purpleD{x^3\, dx=\dfrac{du}{4}}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle \int e^{\blueD{x^4}}\cdot \,\purpleD{x^3\, dx}\,&=\displaystyle \int e^{\blueD u}\, \cdot \purpleD{\dfrac{du}{4}}\,\\\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int e^u\cdot \dfrac14\, du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac14\displaystyle \int e^u\, du\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&=\dfrac14\displaystyle \int e^u\, du~\\\\\\
&=\dfrac14e^u+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=\dfrac14e^u+C\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac14e^{\large x^4}+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int x^3e^{x^4}dx\,= \dfrac14e^{\large x^4}+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{(5+3x^2)^4}\cdot \,6x\, dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=5+3x^2}$, then $\purpleD{du=6x \, dx}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{(\blueD{5+3x^2})^4}\cdot \,\purpleD{6x\, dx}\,=\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\blueD u^4}\, \purpleD{du}\,$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= \int\dfrac{1}{u^4}\,du~\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{3u^3}+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=-\dfrac{1}{3u^3}+C\\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{3(5+3x^2)^3}+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{6x}{(5+3x^2)^4}\,dx\,= -\dfrac{1}{3(5+3x^2)^3}+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{16-x^2}}\cdot \,x\, dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=16-x^2}$, then ${du=-2x \, dx}$ and $\purpleD{x\, dx=-\dfrac{du}{2}}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\blueD{16-x^2}}}\cdot \,\purpleD{x\, dx}\,&=\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\blueD u}}\,\left( \purpleD{-\dfrac{du}{2}}\right)\\\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{ u}}\cdot \left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\,du\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{ u}}\, du\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= -\dfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{ u}}\, du\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2\sqrt u+C\\\\\\
&=-\sqrt{u}+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=-\sqrt{u}+C\\\\\\\
&=-\sqrt{16-x^2}+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{16-x^2}}\,dx\,= -\sqrt{16-x^2}+C$

If we let $\blueD {u=\sin(x)}$, then $\purpleD{du=\cos(x) \, dx}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle \int \blueD{\sin}^3\blueD{(x)}\purpleD{\cos(x)\,dx}
&=\displaystyle \int \blueD u^3\,\purpleD {du}\\\\\\\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= \displaystyle \int u^3\, du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{4}u^4+C\\\\\\\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=\dfrac{1}{4}u^4+C\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac14\sin^4(x)+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int \sin^3(x)\cos(x)\,dx\,= \dfrac14\sin^4(x)+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle \int \cos(5x^2)\cdot \,x\, dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=5x^2}$, then ${du=10x \, dx}$ and $\purpleD{x\, dx=\dfrac{du}{10}}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int \cos(\blueD{5x^2})\,\cdot \purpleD{x\,dx}\,&=\displaystyle \int \cos(\blueD{u})\, \cdot \purpleD{\dfrac{du}{10}}\\\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int \cos(u)\cdot\dfrac{1}{10}\,du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{10}\displaystyle \int \cos(u)\, du\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= \dfrac{1}{10}\displaystyle \int \cos(u)\, du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{10}\cdot \sin(u)+C\\\\\\\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=\dfrac{1}{10}\sin(u)+C\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{10}\sin(5x^2)+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int x\cos(5x^2)\,dx\,=\dfrac{1}{10}\sin(5x^2)+C $
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle \int \tan^2(x)\sec^2(x) \, dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=\tan(x)}$, then $\purpleD{du=\sec^2(x) \, dx}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\displaystyle \int \blueD{\tan}^2\blueD{(x)}\,\purpleD{\sec^2(x)\,dx}\,
=\displaystyle \int \blueD u^2\,\purpleD{du}\\\\\\$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= u^2\, du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{3}u^3+C\\\\\\\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=\dfrac{1}{3}u^3+C\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{3}\tan^3(x)+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int \sec^2(x)\tan^2(x)\,dx\,= \dfrac{1}{3}\tan^3(x)+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $4\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{3+\cos(x)}\,\cdot \sin(x)\,dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=3+\cos(x)}$, then ${du=-\sin(x) \, dx}$ and $\purpleD{-du=\sin(x) \, dx} $ 

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}4\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\blueD{3+\cos(x)}}\,\cdot \purpleD{\sin(x)\,dx}\,
&=4\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\blueD u}\,\purpleD{(-du)}\\\\\\
&=-4\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\ u}\,du\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= -4\displaystyle \int \dfrac{1}{\ u}\,du\\\\\\
&=-4\ln|u|+C\\\\\\\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=-4\ln|u|+C\\\\\\\
&=-4\ln|3+\cos(x)|+C\\\\\\
&=-4\ln(3+\cos(x))+C&& \small{\gray{(3+\cos(x) }}\small\gray{\text{ is always positive})}\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{4\sin(x)}{3+\cos(x)}\,dx\,= -4\ln(3+\cos(x))+C$

If we let $\blueD {u=2x}$, then ${du=2 \, dx}$ and $\purpleD{ dx=\dfrac{du}{2}}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle \int e^{\large\blueD{2x}}\,\purpleD{ dx}\,&=\displaystyle \int e^{\blueD u}\, \purpleD{\dfrac{du}{2}}\,\\\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int e^u\cdot \dfrac12\, du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac12\displaystyle \int e^u\, du\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&=\dfrac12\displaystyle \int e^u\, du~\\\\\\
&=\dfrac12e^u+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=\dfrac12e^u+C\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac12e^{\large 2x}+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int e^{2x}\,dx\,= \dfrac12e^{\large 2x}+C$
Notice that we can rewrite the integral as $\displaystyle\int\sqrt{5+2x^2}\,\cdot x \,dx\,$.

If we let $\blueD {u=5+2x^2}$, then ${du=4x\, dx}$ and $\purpleD{x\,dx=\dfrac{du}{4}}$.  

Substituting gives us:

$\begin{align}\displaystyle\int\sqrt{\blueD{5+2x^2}}\,\cdot \purpleD{x \,dx}\,&=\displaystyle \int\sqrt{\blueD u}\,\,\cdot \purpleD{\dfrac{du}{4}}\,\\\\\\
&=\displaystyle \int\sqrt{ u} \cdot \dfrac14\, du \\\\\\
&=\dfrac14\displaystyle \int\sqrt{ u}\, du\end{align}$

We recognize this antiderivative.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx}&= \dfrac14\displaystyle \int\sqrt{ u}\, du\\\\\\
&=\dfrac14\cdot \dfrac23u\^{\large\frac32}+C\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{6}u\^{\large\frac32}+C\end{align}$

We can now substitute back to find the antiderivative in terms of $x$.

$\begin{align}\phantom{\displaystyle\int\dfrac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}}dx~}&=\dfrac{1}{6}u\^{\large\frac32}+C\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{6}(5+2x^2)\^{\large\frac32}+C\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle\int x\sqrt{5+2x^2}\, \,dx\,=\dfrac{1}{6}(5+2x^2)\^{\large\frac32}+C$
